name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
عَبَسَ وَتَوَلَّىٰٓ (١)
```

```english
He frowned and turned away (1)
```

```arabic
أَن جَآءَهُ ٱلْأَعْمَىٰ (٢)
```

```english
when the blind man approached him. (2)
```

```arabic
وَمَا يُدْرِيكَ لَعَلَّهُۥ يَزَّكَّىٰٓ (٣)
```

```english
And how do you know, maybe he would purify himself, (3)
```

```arabic
أَوْ يَذَّكَّرُ فَتَنفَعَهُ ٱلذِّكْرَىٰٓ (٤)
```

```english
or take admonition, and the admonition would benefit him! (4)
```

```arabic
أَمَّا مَنِ ٱسْتَغْنَىٰ (٥)
```

```english
But as for someone who is wealthy, (5)
```

```arabic
فَأَنتَ لَهُۥ تَصَدَّىٰ (٦)
```

```english
you attend to him, (6)
```

```arabic
وَمَا عَلَيْكَ أَلَّا يَزَّكَّىٰ (٧)
```

```english
though you are not liable if he does not purify himself. (7)
```

```arabic
وَأَمَّا مَن جَآءَكَ يَسْعَىٰ (٨)
```

```english
But as for someone who comes hurrying to you, (8)
```

```arabic
وَهُوَ يَخْشَىٰ (٩)
```

```english
while he fears [Allah], (9)
```

```arabic
فَأَنتَ عَنْهُ تَلَهَّىٰ (١٠)
```

```english
you are neglectful of him. (10)
```

```arabic
كَلَّآ إِنَّهَا تَذْكِرَةٌ (١١)
```

```english
No indeed! These [verses of the Quran ] are a reminder (11)
```

```arabic
فَمَن شَآءَ ذَكَرَهُۥ (١٢)
```

```english
—so let anyone who wishes remember— (12)
```

```arabic
فِى صُحُفٍ مُّكَرَّمَةٍ (١٣)
```

```english
in honoured scriptures, (13)
```

```arabic
مَّرْفُوعَةٍ مُّطَهَّرَةٍۭ (١٤)
```

```english
exalted and purified, (14)
```

```arabic
بِأَيْدِى سَفَرَةٍ (١٥)
```

```english
in the hands of envoys, (15)
```

```arabic
كِرَامٍۭ بَرَرَةٍ (١٦)
```

```english
noble and pious. (16)
```

## (Commentary)

Narrations from Sunni sources state that these verses were revealed concerning the story of Ibn Umm Maktum, the blind man. He came to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) while the Prophet was with a group of the chieftains of Quraysh, conversing with them regarding Islam. The Prophet frowned at him, so Allah the Exalted censured him with these verses. Some reports from Shi‘i sources also allude to this.

However, other Shi‘i narrations state that the one who frowned and turned away was a man from the Umayyad clan who was with the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). When Ibn Umm Maktum entered, the man frowned and scowled, and thus the verses were revealed. A detailed discussion of this will follow in the "Discussion of Narrations," Allah the Exalted willing.

In any case, the purpose of the Surah is to censure those who prefer the wealthy and the affluent over the weak and the destitute among the believers, thereby elevating the people of this world and debasing the people of the Hereafter. The discourse then leads into an allusion to the insignificance of man in his creation and his utter dependence on Divine management, despite his ingratitude for his Lord’s blessings and His magnificent governance of his affairs. It concludes by mentioning the Resurrection and its recompense as a warning. The Surah is Meccan without dispute.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **عَبَسَ وَتَوَلَّى** — **"He frowned and turned away,"** (80:1) meaning he scowled, contracted his face, and turned aside.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَنْ جاءَهُ الْأَعْمى** — **"because the blind man came to him."** (80:2) This is the reason for the aforementioned frowning, with the particle of causation (لام التعليل) being implied.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَما يُدْرِيكَ لَعَلَّهُ يَزَّكَّى أَوْ يَذَّكَّرُ فَتَنْفَعَهُ الذِّكْرى** — **"And what should make you know? Maybe he may purify himself, or take admonition, and the admonition benefit him."** (80:3-4) This is a circumstantial clause (حال) referring to the subject of "frowned and turned away." Purification (التزكي) — *the process of spiritual cleansing through righteous deeds* — follows remembrance (التذكر) — *the act of taking heed and awakening to the true belief*. The benefit of the remembrance is its invitation to purification through faith and righteous deeds (الصالحات).

The summary of the meaning is: He scowled and turned away from the blind man when he came to him, while he did not know that perhaps this blind man who came to him might purify himself through righteous deeds after attaining faith because of his coming and learning—having already taken heed—or he might take heed because of his coming and being admonished by what he learns, such that the remembrance benefits him and he becomes purified.

In these four verses is a severe censure. The severity is increased by the first two verses being in the third person (الغيبة), which implies a turning away from direct address and indicates a heightened disapproval. The latter two verses then shift to the second person (الخطاب), which intensifies the rebuke and establishes the proof through direct confrontation after the initial turning away and unmediated reprimand.

Describing the one who came as "the blind man" (الأعمى) adds further rebuke. When a needy person striving for his need is blind, lacking sight, and his need concerns his religion—driven to strive by the fear of Allah (خشية الله)—it is only fitting that he be shown mercy and given special attention and affection, rather than being met with a scowl and turned away from.

It has been said—based on the view that the one being censured is the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—that the use of the third person initially was out of honor for him, to suggest that the one from whom the frowning and turning away issued was someone other than him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), because such behavior does not befit one like him. Then, the shift to the second person was also out of honor, to provide intimacy (الإيناس) after the initial estrangement and to show attention after the turning away.

However, this does not align with the direct address in His subsequent saying: **أَمَّا مَنِ اسْتَغْنى فَأَنْتَ لَهُ تَصَدَّى** — **"As for him who considers himself self-sufficient, you attend to him,"** (80:5-6) where the censure and rebuke are even more severe than in **عَبَسَ وَتَوَلَّى** (He frowned and turned away), and there is certainly no "intimacy" in it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَمَّا مَنِ اسْتَغْنى فَأَنْتَ لَهُ تَصَدَّى وَما عَلَيْكَ أَلَّا يَزَّكَّى** — **"As for him who considers himself self-sufficient, you attend to him, though you are not liable if he does not purify himself."** (80:5-7) Wealth (الغنى), self-sufficiency (الاستغناء), and their derivatives carry the same meaning as mentioned by al-Raghib. Thus, "he who considers himself self-sufficient" (من استغنى) refers to one who is clothed in wealth and possesses status, leadership, and greatness in the eyes of people, and who is arrogant (الاستكبار) toward following the truth. Allah says: **إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَيَطْغى أَنْ رَآهُ اسْتَغْنى** — **"Indeed man becomes rebellious when he considers himself self-sufficient."** (96:6-7). Attending (التصدي) means to occupy oneself with something by turning toward it and being concerned with its affairs.

In this verse and the following six verses, there is a detailed explanation of the basis for the aforementioned frowning and turning away for which the person was censured. The gist is: You pay attention and turn toward him who considers himself self-sufficient and is arrogant toward following the truth—and you are not liable if he does not purify himself—while you are distracted and turn away from him who strives to purify himself while he fears Allah.

Regarding His saying: **وَما عَلَيْكَ أَلَّا يَزَّكَّى** — **"though you are not liable if he does not purify himself,"** it is said that "what" (ما) is a negation, meaning: There is no blame upon you if he does not purify himself, such that your eagerness for his Islam should drive you to turn away and be distracted from the one who has already submitted (أسلم) in order to attend to him.

It is also said that "what" (ما) is for rhetorical interrogation (الاستفهام الإنكاري), meaning: What is required of you if he does not cleanse himself from disbelief and immorality? You are but a messenger, and your duty is only the clear delivery of the message.

It is also said the meaning is: You do not care if he does not cleanse himself from the filth of disbelief and immorality. This meaning is most suitable for the context of censure, followed by the one before it, and then the first.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَمَّا مَنْ جاءَكَ يَسْعى وَهُوَ يَخْشى فَأَنْتَ عَنْهُ تَلَهَّى** — **"But as for him who comes to you hastening, while he fears [Allah], you are distracted from him."** (80:8-10) Striving (السعي) is to walk quickly. Thus, the meaning of **وَأَمَّا مَنْ جاءَكَ يَسْعى** (But as for him who comes to you hastening), according to the context, is: As for him who comes to you in haste to take heed and purify himself through the religious knowledge he learns.

His saying: **وَهُوَ يَخْشى** (while he fears) means he fears Allah. Fear (الخشية) is the sign of taking heed through the Quran. Allah says: **ما أَنْزَلْنا عَلَيْكَ الْقُرْآنَ لِتَشْقى إِلَّا تَذْكِرَةً لِمَنْ يَخْشى** — **"We did not send down to you the Quran that you should be miserable, but as an admonition for him who fears [Allah],"** (20:2-3) and He says: **سَيَذَّكَّرُ مَنْ يَخْشى** — **"He who fears [Allah] will take admonition."** (87:10).

His saying: **فَأَنْتَ عَنْهُ تَلَهَّى** (you are distracted from him) means you amuse yourself and occupy yourself with someone else. The advancement of the pronoun "you" (أنت) in **فَأَنْتَ لَهُ تَصَدَّى** (you attend to him) and **فَأَنْتَ عَنْهُ تَلَهَّى** (you are distracted from him), as well as the pronouns "to him" (له) and "from him" (عنه) in the two verses, is to record and confirm the censure.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كَلَّا إِنَّها تَذْكِرَةٌ فَمَنْ شاءَ ذَكَرَهُ** — **"No indeed! These [verses] are a reminder. So let him who wishes take admonition from it."** (80:11-12) "No indeed" (كَلَّا) is a deterrent (ردع) against what was censured: the frowning, the turning away, the attending to the self-sufficient, and the distraction from the one who fears.

The pronoun in **إِنَّها تَذْكِرَةٌ** (Indeed, it is a reminder) refers to the Quranic verses or the Quran itself. The feminine pronoun is used because the predicate is feminine. The meaning is that the Quranic verses or the Quran is a reminder (تذكرة) — *an exhortation by which the seeker is admonished* — or a "remembrancer" that brings to mind the truth of belief and action.

His saying: **فَمَنْ شاءَ ذَكَرَهُ** (So let him who wishes take admonition from it) is a parenthetical sentence. The pronoun refers to the Quran or the knowledge it reminds one of. The meaning is: Whoever wishes may remember the Quran or remember what the Quran reminds him of, which is the transition to that which the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — guides toward and preserves in its tablet of true belief and action.

The use of this expression, **فَمَنْ شاءَ ذَكَرَهُ** (So let him who wishes take admonition from it), alludes to the fact that there is no compulsion in the call to remembrance. There is no benefit in it that returns to the caller; rather, the one who benefits is the one who takes heed. So let him choose what he chooses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فِي صُحُفٍ مُكَرَّمَةٍ مَرْفُوعَةٍ مُطَهَّرَةٍ** — **"In honored scripts, exalted and purified,"** (80:13-14). It is stated in *al-Majma‘*: "Scripts (الصحف) is the plural of script (صحيفة). The Arabs call anything written upon a *sahifah*, just as they call it a book (كتاب), whether it be parchment or otherwise."

The phrase **فِي صُحُفٍ** (In scripts) is a predicate after a predicate for "it" (the reminder). Its literal meaning is that it is written in multiple scripts in the hands of the angels of revelation. This weakens the view that "scripts" refers to the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ), as the words of the Exalted never use "scripts" (صحف), "books" (كتب), or "tablets" (ألواح) in the plural to refer to the Preserved Tablet. Similarly weak is the view that "scripts" refers to the books of previous prophets, as this does not align with the clear meaning of His saying: **بِأَيْدِي سَفَرَةٍ** (In the hands of envoys), which serves as an attribute for the scripts.

His saying: **مُكَرَّمَةٍ** (honored) means magnified. **مَرْفُوعَةٍ** (exalted) means high in status with Allah. **مُطَهَّرَةٍ** (purified) means cleansed from the filth of falsehood, vain talk, doubt, and contradiction. Allah says: **لا يَأْتِيهِ الْباطِلُ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَلا مِنْ خَلْفِهِ** — **"falsehood cannot approach it from before it nor from behind it,"** (41:42), and He says: **إِنَّهُ لَقَوْلٌ فَصْلٌ وَما هُوَ بِالْهَزْلِ** — **"It is indeed a decisive word, and it is not a jest,"** (86:13-14), and He says: **ذلِكَ الْكِتابُ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ** — **"This is the Book, there is no doubt in it,"** (2:2), and He says: **وَلَوْ كانَ مِنْ عِنْدِ غَيْرِ اللهِ لَوجَدُوا فِيهِ اخْتِلافاً كَثِيراً** — **"Had it been from [someone] other than Allah, they would surely have found much discrepancy in it."** (4:82).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **بِأَيْدِي سَفَرَةٍ كِرامٍ بَرَرَةٍ** — **"by the hands of envoys, noble and pious."** (80:15-16) These are successive attributes for the scripts. The envoys (السفرة) are the ambassadors, the plural of ambassador (سفير), meaning a messenger. "Noble" (كرام) is an attribute for them regarding their essences, and "pious" (بررة) is an attribute for them regarding their actions, which is excellence in deed.

The meaning of the verses is that the Quran is a reminder written in multiple scripts, magnified, exalted in status, and purified from every stain and filth, in the hands of ambassadors from among the angels who are noble before their Lord due to the purity of their essences and pious before Allah the Exalted due to the goodness of their deeds.

It appears from the verses that there are angels of revelation who are tasked with carrying the scripts and revealing the Quran contained within them. They are the assistants of Gabriel and under his command. Attributing the delivery of revelation to them does not contradict its attribution to Gabriel in verses such as: **نَزَلَ بِهِ الرُّوحُ الْأَمِينُ عَلى قَلْبِكَ** — **"The Trustworthy Spirit has brought it down to your heart,"** (26:193-194). Allah said in his description: **إِنَّهُ لَقَوْلُ رَسُولٍ كَرِيمٍ ذِي قُوَّةٍ عِنْدَ ذِي الْعَرْشِ مَكِينٍ مُطاعٍ ثَمَّ أَمِينٍ** — **"It is indeed the speech of a noble messenger, powerful and established in the presence of the Lord of the Throne, obeyed there and trustworthy."** (81:19-21). Thus, he is obeyed by the angels who act upon his command and bring what he intends. The revelation, which is the act of his assistants, is his act, just as his act and their acts are all the act of Allah. This is similar to how the taking of souls (التوفي), which is the act of the assistants of the Angel of Death, is his act, and his act and their acts are all the act of Allah the Exalted. This discussion has been alluded to repeatedly.

It is also said that "envoys" (سفرة) refers to the scribes among the angels, but the previous meaning is clearer. It is also said they are the reciters who write and read it, but this is as you see (i.e., weak).

## (Discussion of Narrations)

In *al-Majma‘*, it is said: The verses were revealed concerning ‘Abdullah ibn Umm Maktum—who was ‘Abdullah ibn Shurayh ibn Malik ibn Rabi‘ah al-Fihri—from the clan of ‘Amir ibn Lu’ayy.

This was because he came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) while he was conversing with ‘Utbah ibn Rabi‘ah, Abu Jahl ibn Hisham, al-‘Abbas ibn ‘Abd al-Muttalib, Ubayy, and Umayyah ibn Khalaf, calling them to Allah and hoping for their Islam. He said: "O Messenger of Allah, recite to me and teach me from what Allah has taught you." He began calling him and repeating the call, not knowing that the Prophet was occupied and attending to others, until dislike appeared on the face of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) for interrupting his speech. He said to himself: "These chieftains will say: 'His followers are only the blind and the slaves.'" So he turned away from him and attended to the group he was speaking with, and thus the verses were revealed.

After that, the Messenger of Allah used to honor him, and whenever he saw him, he would say: "Welcome to the one concerning whom my Lord censured me," and he would ask him: "Do you have any need?" He appointed him as his deputy over Medina twice during two military expeditions.

**I say:** Al-Suyuti narrated the story in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from ‘Aishah, Anas, and Ibn ‘Abbas with slight variations, and what al-Tabarsi cited is the summary of the narrations.

However, the verses do not clearly indicate that the one intended is the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Rather, it is a mere report that does not explicitly name the subject. In fact, there is that which indicates the one intended is someone else, because frowning is not among the attributes of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) even with his distinct enemies, let alone with the guided believers. Furthermore, the description of attending to the wealthy and being distracted from the poor does not resemble his noble character, as noted by al-Murtada (رحمه الله).

Allah had already magnified his character (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) when He said—and this was before the revelation of this Surah—**وَإِنَّكَ لَعَلى خُلُقٍ عَظِيمٍ** — **"And indeed you possess a great character."** (68:4). This verse occurs in Surah *Nun* (al-Qalam), which narrations detailing the order of revelation agree was revealed after Surah *Iqra’*. How then is it conceivable that Allah would magnify his character at the beginning of his mission and speak of it in absolute terms, only to then return and censure him for some of his character-related actions and disparage him for things like attending to the wealthy even if they disbelieve, and being distracted from the poor even if they believe and seek guidance?

Allah the Exalted also said: **وَأَنْذِرْ عَشِيرَتَكَ الْأَقْرَبِينَ وَاخْفِضْ جَناحَكَ لِمَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** — **"Warn your nearest relations, and lower your wing to the faithful who follow you."** (26:214-215). He commanded him to lower his wing to the believers, and the Surah is among the Meccan Surahs, and the verse is in the context of **وَأَنْذِرْ عَشِيرَتَكَ الْأَقْرَبِينَ** (Warn your nearest relations), which was revealed in the early stages of the call.

Similarly, His saying: **لا تَمُدَّنَّ عَيْنَيْكَ إِلى ما مَتَّعْنا بِهِ أَزْواجاً مِنْهُمْ وَلا تَحْزَنْ عَلَيْهِمْ وَاخْفِضْ جَناحَكَ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ** — **"Do not extend your glance toward what We have provided certain groups of them... and lower your wing to the believers."** (15:88). In the context of this verse is His saying: **فَاصْدَعْ بِما تُؤْمَرُ وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ الْمُشْرِكِينَ** — **"So proclaim what you have been commanded, and turn away from the polytheists."** (15:94), which was revealed at the start of the public call. How then can it be imagined that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) would frown and turn away from the believers when he had been commanded to respect their faith, lower his wing, and not extend his eyes toward the worldly life of the people of this world?

Moreover, the ugliness of preferring the wealth of the wealthy—which is not a criterion for any merit—over the perfection and righteousness of the poor, by frowning and turning away from the poor man and attending to the wealthy man because of his wealth, is an intellectual ugliness (قبح عقلي) that contradicts noble human character. Avoiding it does not require a verbal prohibition.

By this and what preceded it, the answer becomes clear to those who say: Allah the Glorified did not prohibit him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) from this act except at this time, so it would not be a sin for him except after the prohibition, but not before.

This is because the claim that Allah did not prohibit him except at this time is an arbitrary assertion that is rejected. Even if it were granted, the intellect judges its ugliness, and its occurrence would thus contradict noble character. Allah the Exalted had already magnified his character (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) before that when He said: **وَإِنَّكَ لَعَلى خُلُقٍ عَظِيمٍ** (And indeed you possess a great character) and spoke in absolute terms. Character (الخلق) is a stable disposition (ملكة) that does not fail to produce the action appropriate to it.

It is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام)—as in *al-Majma‘*—that these verses were revealed concerning a man from the Umayyad clan who was with the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). When Ibn Umm Maktum came, the man saw him, felt disgusted by him, withdrew into himself, frowned, and turned his face away from him. So Allah the Glorified recounted that and condemned it.

In *al-Majma‘*, it is also narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) that he said: Whenever the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saw ‘Abdullah ibn Umm Maktum, he would say: "Welcome, welcome! By Allah, Allah shall never censure me concerning you again." He would treat him with such kindness that Ibn Umm Maktum would even refrain from coming to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) because of what he did for him.

**I say:** The discussion regarding this is the same as the discussion regarding what preceded it. The meaning of "he would even refrain..." is that he would hold back from attending the Prophet's presence due to the abundance of the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) kindness toward him, out of a sense of being overwhelmed and shy.
